title: Single Sign-On Across 50 Accounts
description:
## Workforce Access at Scale

A company has **50 AWS accounts** in an Organization and roughly 1,500 employees. They already manage employee identities in a corporate **external identity provider** (their HR-integrated IdP supporting SAML/SCIM). Requirements:

- Employees get **single sign-on** to the AWS Console and CLI across all accounts using their existing corporate credentials.
- Access levels (e.g. read-only auditor, developer, admin) are defined **centrally** and assigned to groups, then applied consistently to many accounts.
- No long-lived AWS credentials, and onboarding/offboarding is driven from the corporate IdP.

Which solution fits best?

options.text: Use **IAM Identity Center** (formerly AWS SSO): connect the external IdP, define **permission sets**, and assign groups to accounts for federated SSO across the organization.
Create an **IAM user for every employee in every account** and distribute passwords and access keys.
Create a single shared IAM admin user in the management account and have all employees log in with it.
Generate **long-lived access keys** centrally and email them to each employee for CLI access to all accounts.

explanation: **IAM Identity Center** is the AWS service for **centralized workforce SSO** across an entire Organization. You connect your **external IdP** (SAML for sign-on, SCIM for user/group provisioning), define **permission sets** (reusable role templates like auditor/developer/admin), and **assign groups to accounts** — Identity Center then federates users into each account assuming a role that issues **temporary credentials**, with console *and* CLI support. Onboarding/offboarding flows from the IdP. Option B (an IAM user per employee per account) is precisely the unscalable, unmanageable anti-pattern Identity Center exists to replace — 1,500 × 50 identities to provision and rotate. Option C (one shared admin user) destroys auditability and least privilege. Option D (long-lived access keys emailed out) is a serious security failure: long-lived credentials, no central control, no federation.

hints.title: Federate, don't duplicate
hints.body: If the company already has a corporate IdP and many accounts, the answer is almost always centralized federation, not per-account IAM users.

hints.title: Permission sets are the unit
hints.body: IAM Identity Center applies reusable permission sets to groups across many accounts — that's how you keep access consistent at scale.
